Oh my god.
When I was younger, a lot of roles I was

That's less frequent, though. Something

You sure about that? You sure about

that?
 Oh, really? Please tell us more

I don't know how you coped.
Yes, I can definitely smell shite.
Not to impugn her acting ability or

would have had the career she's had if

It all kind of strikes me as the
Hollywood equivalent of the old you
can't fire me, I quit argument, trying
to get ahead of something that's
inevitably going to happen anyway by
refraraming it as some brave
self-sacrificing decision on your own

actually an interesting pattern of
behavior in these 40something actresses
who all mysteriously get tired of the
male gays just as the male gays is about
to get tired of them. The thing is, I
kind of agree with her, at least in

for any actor to be typ cast and pigeon

to broaden their creative range. But

what can I say?
 It's Hollywood, baby.
It's an industry based around pattern
recognition and branding, selling actors
like their commodities. And it's not a
uniquely female problem in the
slightest. Take Hugh Grant for example.
That guy had to labor away for most of
his career playing fppish, bumbling
British guys in tedious romantic
comedies because that's what people knew
him for. And it's only now in his 60s
that he's finally able to branch out
into more challenging roles. Matthew
McConey, he suffered from the same
problem, trapped in romcom purgatory for
more than a decade before escaping into
more mature roles in his 40s and 50s. Or
how about all those action stars from
the 80s and 90s who tried and failed to
transition into other genres only to
fade out completely in the 2000s when
they aged out of their roles. The sad
reality is that most actors end up being
known for something that ends up
defining the course of their careers.
Whether it's charm and good looks, a
flare for comedy, big muscles, or just
being really attractive. I guess it's
not a good or a bad thing. It's just the
way the world works. The Scarlett
Johansson from 10 or 15 years ago
understood this perfectly well because
she reaped the rewards of being
beautiful and getting lots of
opportunities that other less attractive
actresses probably got passed over for.
Do you think all those young aspiring
stars felt bad for you? Or do you think
they ended up staring long and hard at

that in the great genetic lottery, you
just so happen to have better luck than
them? Do you think they feel bad for you
now as you approach the end of this
particular phase of a career that they
never even got a chance to experience?
But okay, let's move past the angst of a
fading star facing up to the unrelenting
march of time and ask ourselves, is the
male gaze really a thing in movies? Or
is it just a case of Hollywood
understanding their audience and giving
them what they want? Because here's
another uncomfortable truth for you. Us
humans enjoy looking at attractive
people.
It's crazy, I know, but there's a reason
why the biggest and most successful
movie stars throughout history tend to
have been easy on the eye. It's because
we're attracted to them, whether we want
to admit it or not. There's a reason why
male and female stars work so hard to
stay in shape. We like looking at all
those tanned sculpted bodies. It makes
us feel good. It's aspirational. Is it
realistic? Not really. But deep down, we
don't really want it to be. reality is
beer guts and unwanted body hair and
disappointingly pale sagging flesh. If
we want to look at [ __ ] like that, we
only have to look in the mirror. And
like I said earlier, wanting to look at
attractive people on screen isn't a
uniquely male trait. And if anything, it
seems to be skewing in the opposite
direction nowadays. Ever noticed in the
Marvel films how the ladies outfits have
gradually become more padded, bulky, and
concealing like it's the [ __ ] 19th
century again, while every male actor is
still expected to do the obligatory
shirtless scene. I'll tell you why. It's
because every straight woman in the
Western Hemisphere has probably rubbed
one out to shirtless Hemsworth. We all
know it happens, and we all know the
female gaze is something that Hollywood
absolutely caters to and has for a very
long time. But have you ever stopped to
think for one second how much work and
sacrifice and dedication it takes to get
that kind of physique? Because trust me,
whatever diets and exercise plans the
ladies go on to stay trim, it absolutely
pales in comparison to what these guys
have to put their bodies through. Hugh
Jackman famously revealed that he has to
know the exact date of his shirtless
scenes 6 months in advance so he can get
into peak shape. What you're seeing in
those few seconds on screen is basically
the end result of an extremely long and
punishing bodybuilding regime. And as
anyone who's tried something like that
can tell you, it is not [ __ ] fun. But
does anyone complain about it? Not
really. There's no outrage, no
accusations of exploitation, no
complaints about the female gays. They
just kind of get on with it because they
know it's expected of them. So, I guess
what I'm trying to say with all of this
is that generally being a movie star
means you're in the business of looking
good. Whether you want to or not, it's
not really about a male gaze or a female
gaze. It's a human gaze. We like
beautiful people, and if you're lucky
enough to be one and reap the rewards
and opportunities that come with it,
well, maybe don't expect the rest of us
to feel too sorry for you when it's all
over.
Anyway, that's all I've got for today.
Go away now.
